explicit_text: I lived in Arizona for a while, especially on an Indian reservation, and I found it to be a wonderful place. However, when I ran out of money, survival became difficult, so I decided to return to Southern California. Arizona was great for nature and terrain, but I needed to be closer to the sea for swimming and bodysurfing.

explicit_text: Topanga Canyon was the perfect compromise between the sea and Arizona for me. I moved there in the late 1950s because of the cheap rent and the community of artists living there. I lived in a large house on Fernwood Pacific Drive before moving to a beautiful house on the top of the hill. It was a place full of eccentric neighbors like Karl Nolde and Mrs. Buckman with her monkey farm, and it really stimulated my creativity and social life.

explicit_text: Karl Nolde was my neighbor in Topanga Canyon, and he was a German expressionist painter and the brother of Emil Nolde. He was a wonderfully eccentric character who loved old cars and had many wild adventures, including having his cars stolen and getting arrested in Van Nuys. Karl eventually moved to Guanajuato, Mexico, where he continued painting and was honored with a retrospective by the Leipzig Symphony Orchestra.
